description: Name-content conceptual-bridge probe: do the detector's 200 MIN-closest cross-cluster proof pairs (edit_distance_bridges) share domain-specific NAME tokens? Mirrors e-0039 (conceptual_genericity) EXACTLY but swaps the tactic-token bag for a full_name concept-token bag (split on ./_/camelCase, lowercased). NAME-token genericity = number of distinct clusters a token appears in; specific = <=3 clusters, generic = >=30. Same top-30 x top-30-longest selection and random cross-cluster control.
motivation: a-0037/a-0038 ruled out tactic tokens and multi-token motifs and concluded the conceptual-bridge signal lives in statement/type CONTENT 'which the corpus does not expose'. But the corpus DOES expose one content-bearing field: full_name (descriptive Mathlib concept strings). The conceptual-DETECTION thread never tested it (only the retrieval chain used name tokens). Cheap CPU-only check over existing data.
formal_results: 15,664 proofs (>=5 tactics) over 73 clusters; 2,534 distinct NAME tokens. Conceptual-bridge pairs vs random cross-cluster control: frac sharing >=1 NAME token 0.150 vs 0.168; mean shared mass 0.165 vs 0.198; mean generic_frac of shared tokens 0.733 vs 0.675. Pairs sharing >=1 domain-specific (<=3-cluster) NAME token: 0/200 (control rate 0.0002 = floor). Most generic name tokens: is(41), iff(41), eq(34), of(34), map(32), mul(30).
results_description: 0/200 conceptual-bridge pairs share any domain-specific NAME token, at the random floor; they share name tokens slightly LESS than random cross-cluster pairs (15.0% vs 16.8%), and the shared tokens are ubiquitous scaffolding words (is/eq/of/map/mul/le).
conclusions: Even the theorem NAMES -- the one content-bearing field the corpus exposes -- carry no shared domain-specific conceptual signal for the tactic-similarity-selected bridge pairs (in fact slightly below random). Extends a-0037/a-0038 from tactic tokens+motifs to name content: NO field this corpus exposes (tactic tokens, motifs, or names) recovers the conceptual-bridge signal, confirming the gap is a corpus/representation limit requiring statement/type structure the corpus omits.
